title: Rigidity and recurrence in symplectic dynamics
speaker: Matthias Schwarz
affiliation: Universität Leipzig; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Symplectic Geometry and its dynamics originated from classical mechanics as the geometry of physical phase space, in particular from celestial mechanics, and one of the most driving questions is up to today that of stability for such systems. One of the most fundamental features of phase space dynamics is Poincare Recurrence which derives from conservation of phase space volume. However, where is the particular symplectic quality left? In this talk, I will present an answer which connects to the features of symplectic rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian phase space and symplectic invariance
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from classical mechanics, passes from configuration space to cotangent phase space with Hamiltonian equations, defines symplectic manifolds, and derives that Hamiltonian flows preserve the symplectic form and hence Liouville volume.

proof_stages.label: Poisson invariants, integrability, and KAM benchmarks
proof_stages.summary: The Poisson bracket packages conserved quantities; complete integrability gives Arnold-Liouville invariant tori with action-angle coordinates, while KAM/Moser-type results show that Diophantine tori and elliptic fixed-point neighborhoods persist under small perturbations, obstructing ergodicity.

proof_stages.label: Measure recurrence and the symplectic strengthening problem
proof_stages.summary: Poincare recurrence follows from finite volume preservation for any measure-preserving map, but this forgets the full symplectic structure, leading to the question of what more is forced when the map is symplectic.

proof_stages.label: Gromov rigidity through non-squeezing and capacities
proof_stages.summary: Gromov's flexibility-versus-rigidity alternative is resolved by non-squeezing, which yields symplectic capacities and C0-closedness of symplectomorphisms, although known capacities are not additive enough to prove recurrence directly.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian intersection rigidity input
proof_stages.summary: A second rigidity principle is introduced: under compactness and a strong relative homotopy hypothesis, a Hamiltonian image of a Lagrangian must intersect the original Lagrangian, with a homological lower bound in the transverse case.

proof_stages.label: Proposed symplectic Poincare recurrence for local Lagrangian tori
proof_stages.summary: Using Darboux balls and equal-radius product Lagrangian tori, the speaker formulates a work-in-progress claim that every symplectic diffeomorphism has infinitely many special iterated intersections, motivated by ordinary recurrence of balls and their boundaries plus dimension counting.

proof_stages.label: Polydisc and pseudoholomorphic-curve strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proposed proof attaches pseudoholomorphic-curve polydiscs of known volume to the Lagrangian tori, uses volume recurrence to force polydisc intersections, and then invokes symplectic rigidity to infer torus intersections, a route established in the monotone case but obstructed in the nonmonotone case.

examples.name: Rational, irrational, and golden-ratio torus frequencies
examples.note: Torus translations with rationally independent, rationally dependent, and Diophantine/golden-ratio frequencies illustrate dense orbits and which invariant tori are most stable under KAM perturbations.
examples.engagement_reason: he states the qualitative roles of rationally dependent, rationally independent, Diophantine, and golden-ratio frequencies for invariant tori without computing a particular case

passes.reason: rational resonances collapse torus orbits while golden-ratio frequencies are extremally badly approximable, and the surviving Diophantine tori are invoked as a KAM obstruction to ergodicity

examples.name: Kepler planetary motion and the perturbed many-body problem
examples.note: The idealized Sun-planet Kepler model and its many-planet perturbations are presented as the historical source of the stability question leading to persistence of invariant tori.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites Keplerian planetary motion and its perturbed many-body version as the motivating celestial-mechanics example, stating known integrability and perturbation facts without working them out

examples.name: Nonmonotone torus rotated by an SU(2) element
examples.note: A two-radius nonmonotone torus in complex dimension two can be Hamiltonian-rotated so that the intended polydisc-to-torus implication fails, motivating the monotone/equal-radius restriction and the remaining difficulty.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the two-radii nonmonotone torus concretely as a counterexample by rotating it with an SU(2) Hamiltonian element and discussing the resulting failure of the desired polydisc-to-torus intersection implication
